title: Cat Thick Yellow Eye Discharge: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what thick yellow eye discharge in cats means, why it is a medium-urgency concern, and how a photo can help your vet with a fast diagnosis.
content_html:
What is thick yellow eye discharge in cats?

Thick yellow discharge, often referred to by veterinarians as purulent discharge, is typically an indication of a bacterial or viral infection. Unlike clear tears which might be caused by allergies, yellow or green mucus suggests that white blood cells are actively fighting an infection. This is commonly seen in cases of Feline Upper Respiratory Infection (URI), bacterial conjunctivitis, or an infected corneal ulcer.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While thick yellow discharge is rarely life-threatening in the immediate sense, it is often painful and can lead to permanent scarring or loss of vision if left untreated. You should aim to have your cat seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If your cat is also squinting, pawing at the eye, or if the eye appears cloudy, you should seek care as soon as possible to rule out a corneal injury.

Why a photo helps with triage

Providing a high-quality photo of your cat's eye to a triage professional can be incredibly beneficial. A photo allows us to see the exact color and consistency of the discharge and the degree of inflammation in the surrounding tissues. Because cats often squint or hide their eyes when stressed at a clinic, a clear photo taken in a relaxed home environment helps your veterinary team make a more accurate assessment of the severity before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat's yellow discharge?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications on your cat. Many human eye drops contain ingredients that can be harmful or even cause the eye to worsen if an ulcer is present.

faqs.question: Is yellow eye discharge contagious to other cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, many causes of yellow discharge, such as Feline Herpesvirus or Calicivirus, are highly contagious between cats. It is best to isolate the affected cat until they have been evaluated.

faqs.question: How can I safely clean my cat's eye at home?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away the discharge using a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water. Always wipe away from the eye and use a fresh surface for each wipe to avoid spreading the infection.
